name: Flerovium
name_origin: Named in honor of the Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions at JINR Dubna, itself named after Soviet physicist Georgy Flyorov.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) Dubna team led by Yuri Oganessian, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

occurrence.abundance_summary: Flerovium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via hot-fusion reactions such as 48Ca + 244Pu.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: Limited gas-phase chemistry experiments suggest flerovium is a volatile metal with notably low reactivity, possibly intermediate between a heavy post-transition metal and a noble gas due to strong relativistic stabilization of the 7s2 7p1/2 closed subshell. Detailed bulk chemistry remains uncharacterized and the predicted vs. measured volatility is still actively debated.
isotopes_summary: All flerovium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 289Fl with a half-life of a few seconds (around 2-3 s); 287Fl and 288Fl have shorter half-lives and are also used in chemistry experiments.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Flerovium
Yakushev, A. et al. 'Superheavy element flerovium (element 114) is a volatile metal', Inorganic Chemistry, 2014
